ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai-style sliced chicken salad; 'chicken dish' (FOODON_00002670) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai-dressed sliced chicken; A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is used as the closest defensible anchor. Dressing components are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Thai-style sliced chicken salad dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Thai-style sliced chicken is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small plate of sliced poached chicken dressed with Thai salad sauce as served in a Singapore hawker or food court context.

nutrient_source.title: S7 Sliced Chicken — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for the dish identified as 'S7 Sliced Chicken' (cr_id G00010408). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy, Chicken breast, cooked, roasted, item 05064) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions; value estimated at 0.11 g/100g for lean poached chicken breast.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style sliced chicken salad. GI is estimated at 35 based on the analogous food category of lean cooked chicken with low-carbohydrate dressing. Chicken meat itself has negligible starch and a very low GI; the small carbohydrate contribution (2.25 g/100g) comes primarily from the Thai dressing (lime juice, small amounts of sugar). The GI of 35 is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for lean protein-dominant dishes with minimal added sugar. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 2.25 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.18, rounded to 1.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1040 mg/100g — critically elevated, far exceeding the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish in its standard preparation; even a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1560 mg sodium, representing 78% of the 2000 mg/day recommended limit — request the dressing on the side or substitute with plain poached chicken.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.25 g/100g) and high protein (16.8 g/100g) support postprandial glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable as a protein component for people with Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible sugar (0.2 g/100g) and very low carbohydrate content produce a minimal glycemic response, but it should be paired with low-GI carbohydrates rather than white rice, and sodium intake from the dressing must be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium critically high at 1040 mg/100g; fat and saturated fat are very low, which is cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lean protein profile (fat 0.39 g/100g, saturated fat ~0.11 g/100g) and low cholesterol (36 mg/100g) are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the very high sodium content poses a significant cardiovascular risk and patients should request reduced-sodium dressing or consume only a small portion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (garlic, ginger) are beneficial, but very high sodium may worsen tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The lean poached chicken and anti-inflammatory dressing aromatics (garlic, ginger, lemongrass) are beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis, but the high sodium content (1040 mg/100g) may exacerbate joint swelling and should be reduced by requesting sauce on the side.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai-Style Sliced Chicken Salad (known in Singapore hawker contexts as 'S7 Sliced Chicken') consists of tender, thinly sliced poached or steamed chicken breast tossed in a tangy, spicy Thai salad dressing typically made from soy sauce, fish sauce, lime juice, garlic, ginger, chilli, and lemongrass. The dish is served chilled or at room temperature and is characterised by a bright, zesty, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a tender, moist texture. It is a popular protein dish in Singapore food courts and hawker centres, reflecting the strong Thai culinary influence in the local food landscape. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (16.8 g/100g), very low in fat (0.39 g/100g), and extremely low in carbohydrates (2.25 g/100g), making it an excellent protein source for weight management and blood glucose control. However, the sodium content is critically high at 1040 mg/100g due to the soy sauce and fish sauce base of the dressing, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease unless the dressing is significantly reduced. The dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free. It is typically consumed as part of a mixed hawker meal at lunch or dinner, paired with rice or noodles.
common_user_queries: Is S7 sliced chicken good for diabetics?
How much sodium is in Thai sliced chicken?
Is Thai chicken salad low carb?
Can I eat sliced chicken with hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010408) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy for lean poached chicken breast as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category for lean protein-dominant dishes.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; the dish is a lean protein preparation with no plant fibre components in the base chicken. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Thai-dressed sliced chicken dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact preparation as of 2026-04-16. 3. Saturated fat (0.11 g/100g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy item 05064 (Chicken breast, cooked, roasted) as the closest approved-source equivalent for lean poached chicken; this is documented as an adapted value. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Sodium at 1040 mg/100g is critically high and is the primary clinical concern for this dish across hypertension and cardiovascular disease contexts. 6. Multilingual local names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 requirements for a Singapore-served dish with Thai cuisine classification. All local-language strings verified present verbatim in the flat aliases array.

All new local-language strings added verbatim to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language. Verified aliases consistency self-check passes.
